summary: Gruber reviews the iPad Mini after a week of daily use, finding it an exceptional device held back only by its non-retina display. The hardware design is more elegant than the full-size iPad, the weight makes it a joy to hold one-handed, and performance matches the iPad 3. He argues the non-retina screen is only a real complaint for those already accustomed to retina, and won't matter to the mass market. Despite his personal attachment to retina displays, he's switching to the Mini because the size and weight advantages are that compelling. He predicts the Mini will become the bestselling iPad model, much like the iPod Mini overtook the original iPod.
key_points: The iPad Mini's hardware design (chamfered edges, less tapered back, metal buttons) is more elegant than the full-size iPad, and its weight makes one-handed use effortless
The non-retina display is the sole significant complaint, but only matters to those already using retina — the iPad 2's continued strong sales prove non-retina isn't a dealbreaker for most buyers
Apple kept the iPad 2 in the lineup because it's still selling well, and they don't know whether customers want the cheapest iPad or the cheapest full-size iPad
Performance matches the iPad 3 in benchmarks, which is impressive for a $329 device in a much smaller form factor
The Mini changes the optimal travel kit calculus: pair a bigger MacBook with the smaller iPad rather than carrying two similarly-sized devices
money_quotes: My ideal iPad would be a Mini with a retina display.
I prefer the Mini over the full-size iPad in every single regard other than display resolution, and though I (and many of you) obsess over display resolution, it's not an issue in the mass market.
If it weren't selling well, Apple would have dropped it from the lineup. But because it is selling well, they're keeping it in the lineup, because they don't know why it's selling well.
Going non-retina is a particularly bitter pill for me, but I like the iPad Mini's size and weight so much that I'm going to swallow it.
The reduced bezel width along the sides poses no problem; the device is light enough that you don't need a place to rest your thumb on the surface.
I think there are going to be a staggering number of iPad Minis in Santa's sack this year.
My guess is that this is going to play out much like the iPod and iPod Mini back in 2004: the full-size model will continue to sell strongly, but the Mini is going to become the bestselling model.
key_insight: The iPad Mini's size and weight advantages are so compelling that they override even the painful loss of retina display quality, signaling it will become the dominant iPad model.
